g.text: AI Search Techniques for Goal-Directed Problem Solving

g.data-narration: A problem solving agent uses search to find action sequences that transform initial states into goal states.
g.text: What is a Problem Solving Agent?

g.data-narration: These agents power applications from game playing to robotics navigation and automated reasoning systems.
Every well-defined problem has five essential components: initial state, goal state, operators, state space, and path cost.
g.text: Five Components of a Well-Defined Problem
Problem = (S, s₀, A, G, c) where:
S = State Space | s₀ = Initial State | A = Operators | G = Goal | c = Path Cost

g.data-narration: Problem formulation defines these five components to represent real-world problems for AI search algorithms.
Uninformed search explores state space without problem-specific knowledge. BFS, DFS, and UCS are key examples.
g.text: No additional knowledge about states - can only distinguish goal from non-goal.

g.data-narration: Informed search uses heuristics to guide search toward goals efficiently. Greedy Best-First and A* are key examples.
g.text: Uses problem-specific knowledge (heuristic) to guide search toward goal.
Expands node closest to goal: f(n) = h(n)
Combines g(n) + h(n): f(n) = g(n) + h(n)

g.data-narration: Heuristics estimate cost from any state to goal. Admissible heuristics never overestimate true cost, enabling optimal A*.
g.text: h(n) = estimated cost from state n to nearest goal state.
h(n) ≤ cost(n→n') + h(n') for all successors

g.data-narration: We evaluate search algorithms by completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity. These metrics guide algorithm selection.
For 8-puzzle, BFS finds shortest solution but explores many states. A* with Manhattan heuristic dramatically reduces exploration.
Route finding uses city distances. A* with straight-line heuristic finds optimal paths efficiently by focusing on promising routes.
g.text: Find shortest path from Arad to Bucharest.

g.data-narration: The vacuum cleaner demonstrates problem formulation with location and dirt states, showing how AI represents real-world cleaning tasks.
Problem solving agents use search to find goal-achieving action sequences. Choose BFS for shortest path, A* for optimal efficiency.
Remember: Know five components, BFS vs DFS trade-offs, A* optimality conditions, complexities, and heuristic selection criteria.
g.text: Memorize the five components | BFS vs DFS trade-offs | A* needs admissible heuristic for optimality |
